subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Accurate AD/AS and Phillips curve diagrams with correctly labelled curves, equilibrium points and shifts
Full and coherent verbal walkthrough linking diagram movements to policy changes and article data (inflation targets)
weaknesses: Minor typographical errors (e.g., âADIâ instead of âAD1â) and missing clear axis labels
Second diagram misnumbered/missing figure reference, slightly hindering readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student includes two relevant, accurately drawn and fully labelled diagrams and provides a comprehensive, precise explanation of each shift in the diagrams tied directly to the articleâs policy context. Despite minor labelling typos and figure numbering issues, the work meets all descriptors for band 3.

strengths: Consistent use of precise economic terminology such as âcontractionary monetary policy,â âtransmission mechanism,â and âaggregate demandâ
Terminology is correctly and appropriately applied throughout the commentary
weaknesses: Occasional redundancy in phrasing (e.g., repeated references to the âtarget rate of two per centâ)
Could diversify vocabulary further by introducing synonyms like âdisinflationâ or âinflation objectiveâ
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Throughout the commentary the student employs appropriate and accurate economic terminology in context with no misuse, fully satisfying the top band for terminology.

strengths: Thorough application of monetary policy theory with consistent use of AD/AS and Phillips curve frameworks
Integration of realâworld data and article quotations to ground theoretical analysis
Nuanced insight into banking market competition and its effect on the transmission mechanism
weaknesses: Minor misstatement of openâmarket operations mechanism (buying vs. selling) could confuse readers
Analysis could be further deepened by discussing time lags and quantitative evidence
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student applies relevant theory throughout, linking monetary policy, diagram shifts and economic outcomes with multiple well-developed analytical chains and real data. Despite a small factual slip and room for deeper quantitative treatment, the work demonstrates effective, thorough analysis consistent with band 3.

weaknesses: No IB key concept is explicitly identified or defined
No linkage between a key concept and the Bank of Canadaâs policy choice is provided
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The commentary fails to name or explain any IB key concept, and therefore cannot earn marks for this criterion.

strengths: Provides balanced judgments weighing benefits (lower inflation, improved competitiveness) against costs (higher unemployment, poverty)
Logical reasoning supports each judgment, linking interest-rate changes to macroeconomic and fiscal outcomes
weaknesses: Lacks supporting statistical evidence (e.g., unemployment or poverty data) to substantiate claims
Final evaluation paragraph is truncated, limiting depth of long-term impact discussion
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student makes effective and balanced judgments supported by coherent reasoning, but the evaluation is not fully developed with empirical evidence or complete discussion, fitting band 2.

strandSelections.reasoning: The analysis links high rates to lower C and I, lower AD, reduced inflation, and higher unemployment, then discusses consequent effects on purchasing power, competitiveness, poverty and government budget. These chains are accurate and mostly logical, but depth is limitedânumerical evidence, time-frame considerations, and alternative channels are missing. Hence analysis is âappropriate but could be more developed.â

The commentary contains two clearly-drawn, relevant macroeconomic diagrams (AD/AS and short-run Phillips curve). Axes, curves (AD, AD1, LRAS, SRAS; Phillips curve), equilibrium points (APL, APL1, Y, Y1; A, B, U1, U2) and directional arrows are all present and correctly labelled, and the movements shown match standard textbook analysis of contractionary policy. Minor typographical imperfections (e.g. âAD1â printed as âADIâ) do not impede accuracy, so a solid but not exceptional performance is evident.
Because no key concept is identified, there is necessarily no articulated link between that concept and the chosen article. Therefore the strand earns zero.

Relevant theory (monetary policy tools, AD/AS framework, Phillips curve, transmission mechanism) is applied consistently to the articleâs scenario. However, treatment stops at the standard textbook mechanisms; there is no deeper exploration of lags, expectations, or supply-side channels that would constitute a âthoroughâ application. Thus the work fits the âapplied throughoutâ descriptor rather than the top band.

Each judgment is supported by economic reasoning (e.g., higher interest rates raise saving incentives â reduced AD â lower inflation; higher unemployment â lower tax revenue and higher welfare spending). Reasoning is logical but not extensively developed with data or counter-arguments, matching the descriptor for effective but not exemplary support.

The IA never explicitly identifies one of the IB key concepts (e.g., âinterventionâ, âeconomic well-beingâ, âchangeâ). Without such explicit identification, the requirement for this strand is not met.

The student gives a full verbal walkthrough of what each diagram shows: how a maintained high policy rate lowers C and I, shifts AD left, reduces APL and real GDP, and how this translates into a movement along the Phillips curve from higher to lower inflation with higher unemployment. Links to specific article data (3 % inflation target, 2 % goal) reinforce the explanation. The description is accurate, sequential and complete, easily meeting the highest descriptor.

Throughout the 800-word piece the student consistently employs appropriate economic terminologyââcontractionary monetary policy,â âopen-market operations,â âaggregate demand,â âderived demand for labour,â âtransmission mechanism,â etc.âand uses these terms correctly in context. Misuse is not observed, satisfying the top band.

The student weighs potential benefits (lower inflation, stronger price signals, higher competitiveness) against costs (lower GDP, higher unemployment, poverty, fiscal strain) and offers a brief overall judgment (âOverall, the contractionary monetary policyâ¦ has many positive and negative consequencesâ). This constitutes balanced judgement, though not deeply nuanced, so the mid-upper band fits.

annotations.comment: The student effectively integrates direct quotations from the article to frame the policy context, demonstrating strong application of realâworld data to theoretical analysis.
The description of open-market operations reverses the mechanism: selling securities reduces the money supply, not buying. This misstatement could confuse readers.
Deepen the analysis by discussing potential time lags in the transmission mechanism and their effect on both inflation and output trajectories.
The statement linking decreased real GDP to increased poverty lacks specific data and would benefit from quantitative evidence.
Incorporate recent unemployment or income distribution statistics to substantiate the poverty and social unrest claims.
The concluding summary provides an effective and balanced judgment, summarizing the trade-offs between inflation reduction and economic growth.
Elaborate on potential long-term impacts, such as changes in inflation expectations or structural unemployment effects, to deepen the evaluation.
Terminology is consistent, but alternate phrasing (e.g., âdisinflationâ vs. âreduce inflationâ) might enhance variety and precision.
Revise the description of open-market operations to reflect that selling government securities reduces the money supply and raises interest rates.
The commentary consistently uses precise monetary policy terms such as âopen-market operationsâ and âtransmission mechanism,â enhancing clarity and technical accuracy.
The AD/AS diagram is accurately drawn with correctly labelled curves and equilibrium shifts, effectively illustrating the contractionary policy impact.
Consider adding clear axis labels (e.g., âPrice Level (APL)â and âReal GDP (Y)â) to enhance diagram readability.
The text references âFigure 2â for the Phillips curve shift, yet the second diagram is missing or misnumbered, hindering comprehension.
The conceptual link between reduced AD in Figure 1 and movement along the Phillips curve is correctly articulated, showing good theoretical application.
The final sentence of this paragraph is truncated mid-thought, resulting in an incomplete evaluation of the disinflation benefits.
Complete this paragraph by fully outlining how reduced inflation improves material living standards, perhaps with a brief numerical example.
The consistent use of terminology such as âcontractionary monetary policyâ and âaggregate demandâ demonstrates a professional academic tone.
A key IB concept (e.g., âinterventionâ or âeconomic well-beingâ) should be explicitly identified to meet the IA requirements.
Explicitly state and define a key concept, then clearly link it to the Bank of Canadaâs policy choice for full rubric compliance.
The evaluation of positive welfare effects, including improved price signaling and resource allocation, is well-developed and balanced.
A nuanced recognition of the limitation of relying on commercial banks in a competitive lending market adds depth to the analysis.
Clarify the causal logic in the poverty chain by distinguishing short-term income effects from longer-term welfare costs.
Consider discussing alternative policy tools (e.g., macroprudential measures) that the BOC could employ alongside interest-rate adjustments.
Expand on how differences in bank lending spreads or reserve requirements could alter the transmission mechanismâs effectiveness.
Replace repeated terms like âtarget rate of two per centâ with synonyms (e.g., âinflation objectiveâ) to avoid redundancy.
